sentences.text: Absolutely no resistance whatsoever.
Because everybody thought they were going to, to a camp to work for Germany.
And everybody came with their suitcases and their knapsacks, all dressed up, took all their belongings with them.
There was no resistance whatsoever.
They went like cattle.
Yes, that's when I started to think, you know, what is going on?
Why doesn't she want me to stay with her?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I mean, I saw them standing in the school, and I saw the Germans guarding the people with their-- with their guns.
I knew that we were not just going to a work camp, like-- like they said we were going to do.
I think-- there was something else was going on I realized at that-- at that particular time.
I didn't know what, but I knew this was not something usual to-- to bring people to go to work.
Well, most of the people who lived in that particular street, they were all Jewish people.
But before that, we lived in another street, and the people there, they were-- most of them were anti-Semitic.
And we lived in a-- in an apartment, on the-- in Belgium, you say first floor.
Here, you say second floor.
And the people downstairs, they belonged to a party sympathizing with the Nazis.

And every time my father came down the stairs to go to work, or when my mother went down the stairs, or me, or my brother, whoever-- they were waiting for us downstairs, giving us names, yelling and screaming until my father decided, that's enough.
We moved to the-- the other street, [INAUDIBLE] which was a Jewish neighborhood.
But the attitude in that-- in that particular time, where we lived there, was not very pleasant.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the people we knew, they-- they shied away-- didn't want to be too close to us because, you know, you shouldn't be socializing with a Jew.
Well, the man who ran the place, he, lived in Westmalle as well.
Yes, his name was Gustaaf De Cock.
And he told me it would be better for me not to be in the same place with my brother.
So he decided to keep me in his home.
So he took me to his home.
He had nine children of his own, lived in a big, big house.
And the first thing he told me when we came into the house when I stayed there, he said there was one condition that he would take care of me.
And that condition was that I should be converted to Catholicism.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So he educated me in Catholicism himself.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And of course, everything that was based on Catholicism, in his point of view, was the fact that the Jews were being punished for the crucifixion of Jesus.
And he said to me, for me, the perfect thing was to be a devout Catholic, and pray, and maybe I can get the sins away from, from my parents which they had inherited because of the crucifixion.
So they could be returned safe after the war.
Of course, I was a little 12-year-old boy, who has never been exposed to any kind of religion.
So I put my whole heart in studying the Catholic religion.
And I was baptized in-- on Easter day, 1943.
I was a devout Catholic, and I prayed real, real, real hard.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I prayed with such conviction that sometimes I was crying during my prayers.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But before that I was baptized, my brother got his notice to go to Germany, to go to a work camp.
So he thought it wasn't safe for me to be in his house anymore, and he put me back in the preventorium.
And there he kept me for over a year.
While I was there, I was being educated again by a Catholic priest into the religion until I was baptized on Easter day in 1943.
And I stayed in that preventorium until August 1943.
Because again, it was getting a little bit sticky in there because they had taken away the Jewish boy-- the other Jewish boy who was hiding-- the Germans had come in and they had taken him away.
So they decided to move me out and back into the house where they lived with the big family.
I think his motive-- his biggest motive was to save a soul.
To convert me to Catholicism was a triumph.
My brother also was baptized, by the way, for the same reason.
He was baptized way before I was baptized.
But that was-- I think this was his ultimate goal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] He boasted about it, you know?
I have two Jewish boys, and I converted them to Catholicism.
It's hard to say.
I was constantly punished.
I was constantly hit.
If I didn't listen to, to something that I was supposed to do, I was hit.
And he always hit me in my face.
He was left-handed.
Sometimes he would hit me whatever he could find.
But one instance, they had a wooden bench outside in the backyard, and one of the kids that he had has been hammering nails in it.
And when he came home at night, he wanted to know who did it.
And they said, you know, the Jewish boy did.
So he came to me and he said, did you do it?
I said no, I didn't.
He said, you're lying.
I said, no, I'm not lying.
So he kept pushing it.
He kept pushing it.
And he told me to put my hands on the kitchen sink.
And he said, I will teach you not to lie anymore, and he had a bamboo stick.
And he hit me over the knuckles with the bamboo stick, and I was bleeding, until his wife told him, please stop.
He wouldn't stop.
I was sent up to bed without dinner.
When I came home with a bad grade from school-- usually the bad grades I had were in math, or in behavior.
I was a practical joker in school.
Also, he sent me to a private school, which was a Catholic school, so I would get more educated in Catholicism.
So when I came home, and I had a bad grade, I was sent to bed without dinner.
Or he would hit-- he would hit me senseless.
As a matter of fact, I was hit so many times, and so hard, that I lost my hearing in my right ear-- he killed a nerve.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I had a problem, because I was watering my bed-- urinating.
And I did it at home, and I did it over there, too.
And every morning when I came up, I was, of course, I was wet, and he would slap me silly.
And he said I was too lazy to go to the restroom.
So they took my mattress away, and he put a piece of wood on the bed-- and that's what they let me sleep on.
I slept on that for almost two years.
I had to do all of the chores in the house.
And I had to-- they had two floors-- three, three floors.
And upstairs were all the bedrooms, and they had the, the pots that you urinated in during the night.
I had to empty those every morning, clean them out, bring them back.
I had to make the beds, peel potatoes, chop wood for the stove, polish shoes every Saturday-- 25 pair of shoes.
If there was any dirt on the shoes, he would grab the shoe and hit me in my face.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And he could be so-- so lovely, sometimes, too, all of a sudden.
I remember at one time that he would to call me and he said-- because I had a good voice as a child.
I had a soprano voice, and I was part of the chorus to sing in church.
And I was singing soprano.
And he would sit in his chair at home, and he would call me and he said, I want you to sing for me.
My brother always said, when he heard that story, you know, like King Saul calling for David.
And I would sing mostly religious songs.
And at one time, his wife came in, and she was mad.
I don't know what happened, but she grabbed a milk can, and she hit me over the head with it while I was singing, yelling, don't you have anything better to do?
She didn't know that he was sitting, listening to me there.
So he got up, and he beat the heck out of her.
[LAUGHS]
The children, they were-- I wouldn't say anti-Semitic because they didn't know any better.
But of course, I was a Jew, and that means that I crucified Jesus.
So I was always called the dirty Jew.
I didn't even know that they knew what they were saying because they were too young for that.
He had a particular attitude of, of watching me bathe.
Once a week, we had to take a bath in one of those tin tubs.
And he had a daughter who was two years younger than I was, and he was-- he put her in the same tub with me.
And he was watching while this was happening.
He was wondering if anything would happen.
And then you he would come over to me at night, and he would say that he has never seen a boy who was circumcised.
And then he would tell me, also, tried to explain about erections.
But when I asked him about particular questions about sex, he would tell me it was a sin to think about.
And if I wanted to know more about it, I should talk to the priest because the priest would know exactly how to explain it to me.
Which they never did I had to find out in the street.
He was a weird man, to say the least.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was there until 1949.
In 1945, when the war was over, he wanted me to go and be a priest.
And he sent me to a Jesuit school in Turnhout-- outside Westmalle-- to a seminary.
And I got a scholarship there.
And I was a good student.
I was an A student.
For some reason, they, they knew that I was not priest material.
So after about two years, they decided, you know, that it was no use for me to go on.
So when I did my finals, after the finals were over, he came to pick me up.
And he sent me away to the ocean for a couple of months to a family who was doing this as a charity to take care of displaced persons.
And I was there for about two months.
And then he called me home again.
And he said, I had to go and get a job.
I couldn't go back to school anymore.
And it's about time that I should earn some money to support-- help support the family.
So he got me a job in Westmalle in a local bakery.
And at night, I was learning how to bake bread, and during the day I was delivering the bread.
And I did that for about one year.
After that one year, I got a job for a man who had a camera store in Antwerp.
And he asked me one time when I met him, he said, are you interested in photography?
I said, I don't even know what photography is.
He said, well, I'm looking for somebody I can teach how to print.
Would you be interested?
I said, I don't know.
I said, I have to talk to, to this man.
We had to call him [FLEMISH] that was Flemish for father.
So I said, you have to ask [FLEMISH].
So when he heard that the guy was paying me a nice salary, of course, I got the job.
So I was traveling to Antwerp every day.
And this man taught me how to print.
And I did that for about a year, 1948.
And then he told me to quit.
And he said, we need you here because my wife is pregnant, and you have to do the chores in the house.
He had a daughter.
She was two years younger, but she was going to a high school, private high school-- a nun school.
So I had to do all the chores because she was pregnant.
So I did that for about a year or so, before-- not even quite a year because she was still pregnant.
No.
No, there was nobody.
My brother was in Germany.
And I was the only one.
They were all deported.
The only uncle I had with his family, who lived in Antwerp, they were deported at the same time that my parents were deported.
And the rest of the family lived in Holland.
There was no way that I could have contacted anybody.
Oh, absolutely.
That's why I was praying so hard.
No, there was no place to write to.
I couldn't write.
I was hiding.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I remember in another instance that I forgot to mention, is that at one time, they a huge backyard, and they had a couple of cherry trees.
And I was picking cherries one day.
And when I came down from the tree, I went inside the house with my basket full of cherries, and he told me to go into the basement.
I said, why?
He said, just go.
So I went down in the basement.
I found out the next day that the Gestapo had come to the house next door.
We lived at number 29.
And he had searched the whole house, number 27.
They were looking for me.
And I was sitting in the cherry tree, picking cherries.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
But besides that, there was nobody that I contacted.
The people in the village itself, they didn't know I was Jewish.
They thought I was a devout Catholic, especially when they found out I was going to be a priest.
Oh, well we had-- everything was taken care of.
We had good food there.
And all we did there was eat and rest, nothing else.
And I had to study the Catholic religion.
But I was-- I was treated fair in that preventorium because there were too many people there.
He was not the only manager.
They had three managers there, and he was one of them.
It starts when I went to into his house.
That's when the abuse started.
No, no.
[SIGHS] I didn't know where to go.
Where would I go?
I remembered when I ran away, after I saw my mother the last time, that I had no place to go because nobody wanted me there.
Where would I go?
Not only that, he had appointed-- or he was appointed, I don't know how this ever came to pass.
It was never explained to me.
He-- he was my legal guardian.
So legally, I couldn't do anything, run away, because he would have just sent the police after me and bring me back.
No.
I always kept hoping.
No, because I remember when my brother came back after the war was over, I kept saying, you know, pretty soon, any day now.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And my brother, he went every day in Antwerp-- he lived in Antwerp, he didn't live with the family.
After he came back from, from Germany, he got a job in a bank in Antwerp.
And he went every day to check the Jewish register from all the people who came back from the camps, every day.
[PAUSES 4 SECONDS] And that's when we finally started to realize, you know, we won't never see them again.
Praying.
[LAUGHS] I was a devote prayer.
I prayed.
I prayed, I prayed.
That was the only thing I could do.
Or cry by myself at night when I went to bed.
I had a room way upstairs at the top of the building, in the attic, and that was where I had my peace, when I was in bed.
[SIGHS] He bought the radio, and we were listening to the BBC at night from England.
And he bought a huge map, and put it in-- he had a little room which he called the office.
And when the Allies landed in 1944, on D-day, he started to make-- he started to paint the map in red, as they came closer and closer to Belgium.
And then also on the Russian side, he painted it red to see how the war was nearing an end.
And that's, that's how I found out.
You know, that's how I knew that it was going to be over one of these days, not too long.
And we were liberated in September of 1944, and I believe it was Montgomery's troops-- or he was in charge, Montgomery.
We were liberated by the Allies in 19-- September 4, 1944.
[SIGHS] I remember the tanks rolling in the street.
And people standing outside and yelling and screaming and waving, and the soldiers throwing chocolate, chewing gum, cigarettes.
People running out on the streets and grabbing the girls who were friendly with the Germans-- shaved all their hair, tarred them.
Yeah.
They made a public thing out of it.
They put them on a podium and they shaved their hair in public.
Well, for me the war was over, of course.
Well, I didn't have too much of a hardship during the-- because of the war itself.
I had a hardship because of the way I was being treated.
But when the war was over, I kept hoping that my parents would come, and I would go back and have my life the way it used to be before the war, which never happened.
